skill.name: azure-hosted-copilot-sdk
skill.description: Build, configure, and deploy GitHub Copilot SDK applications on Azure using the Azure Developer CLI, Copilot SDK templates, and Azure model provider settings.
skill.summary: Guides GitHub Copilot SDK app scaffolding, Azure deployment, and bring-your-own-model configuration.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and credential patterns. Review found these are mostly Markdown deployment instructions for Azure and GitHub Copilot SDK workflows, not executable hidden code. The skill is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it intentionally guides CLI execution and token handling.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run Azure Developer CLI, Azure CLI, Docker, curl, and deployment workflow commands. This is legitimate for the stated Azure deployment purpose, but it can modify cloud resources or local projects if executed without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and are part of the intended deployment workflow. They are not hidden or obfuscated, but their effects depend on the user environment and cloud subscription.

medium_findings.title: Credential Handling in Deployment Flow
medium_findings.description: The skill documents GitHub token, Azure bearer token, managed identity, and environment variable handling. The pattern is expected for Copilot SDK deployment, but mishandling these values can expose secrets or grant unintended cloud access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential references are explicit and semantically connected to authentication. The surrounding guidance includes legitimate Key Vault and managed identity use, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are GitHub documentation links, Azure endpoint patterns, and localhost test examples. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates data to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible documentation or local test endpoints. No code path sends secrets or collected data to an unknown service.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Signal Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the reviewed lines describe Copilot SDK encryption support, Docker troubleshooting, or skill metadata. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner signal does not match the semantic context of the lines reviewed. Confidence is not higher because the static analyzer did not provide exact matched text for the weak-crypto rule.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Deployment Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands and workflow steps that can create, modify, or deploy Azure resources. Users should review target directories, Azure subscription context, and generated infrastructure before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command guidance is explicit and directly tied to provisioning or inspecting cloud resources. It is appropriate for the skill purpose but should be treated as sensitive operational behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Project File Inspection and Copying
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the assistant to inspect project indicator files and copy infrastructure, token scripts, and deployment manifests into a user project. This is expected integration behavior but can overwrite or expose project structure if done carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem interactions are clearly described in the skill documentation. The guidance includes a warning to scaffold in a temporary directory, reducing but not eliminating operational risk.

content.user_title: Build Copilot SDK Apps on Azure
content.value_statement: Teams need a repeatable path for building Copilot SDK services and hosting them on Azure. This skill guides scaffolding, model configuration, deployment, and integration with existing applications.
actual_capabilities: Routes requests between new Copilot SDK projects, existing app integration, and Azure deployment tasks.
Guides use of the azure-samples Copilot SDK service template with azd init.
Explains GitHub default, GitHub model-specific, and Azure bring-your-own-model configuration paths.
Documents required Azure provider settings, environment variables, and token refresh behavior.
Provides deployment adaptation steps for existing Copilot SDK applications.
Lists practical testing commands and common errors for local and Azure-hosted apps.
limitations: It is guidance content, not a complete application generator by itself.
It assumes access to Azure, GitHub CLI, Azure Developer CLI, and Docker where needed.
It does not replace review of generated Bicep, Dockerfiles, or deployment manifests.
It focuses on Copilot SDK apps and does not cover general Azure Functions or Foundry agent hosting.

use_cases.title: Scaffold a New Copilot SDK Service
use_cases.description: Create a starter Copilot SDK service with API, web UI, infrastructure, and deployment workflow guidance.

use_cases.title: Deploy an Existing SDK App
use_cases.description: Add Azure infrastructure and azd deployment structure to an existing Copilot SDK application.

use_cases.title: Configure Azure BYOM
use_cases.description: Use Azure OpenAI or Azure AI Foundry deployments with DefaultAzureCredential and supported model settings.

prompt_templates.title: Start a New App
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a new GitHub Copilot SDK service for Azure. Show the setup steps and explain what files the template provides.

prompt_templates.title: Add SDK to My App
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this project and add a Copilot SDK chat endpoint using the existing server structure. Do not create a separate server.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Azure Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt my existing Copilot SDK app for Azure deployment. Preserve my app code and explain each infrastructure change before applying it.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Azure Model Provider
prompt_templates.prompt: Configure this Copilot SDK service to use my Azure OpenAI deployment with DefaultAzureCredential, fresh tokens per request, and the correct API settings.

output_examples.input: Build a new Copilot SDK app for Azure.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan that initializes the official template, identifies the API and web services, explains the included infrastructure, and lists local validation steps.

output_examples.input: Deploy my existing Copilot SDK app to Azure.
output_examples.output: A deployment adaptation checklist covering temporary scaffolding, copied infrastructure, azure.yaml updates, token flow, Dockerfile needs, and validation commands.

output_examples.input: Use my Azure OpenAI model with the SDK.
output_examples.output: A configuration guide covering model selection, provider type, Azure endpoint format, managed identity variables, token refresh, and common BYOM errors.

best_practices: Review target directories and cloud subscription context before running deployment commands.
Use managed identity and Key Vault for production secrets instead of hardcoded tokens.
Keep template infrastructure unless a project requirement clearly needs a change.
anti_patterns: Do not run azd init inside an existing project when the workflow calls for temporary scaffolding.
Do not store GitHub or Azure tokens directly in source files or committed configuration.
Do not use unsupported Azure models for encrypted Copilot SDK BYOM traffic.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build and deploy GitHub Copilot SDK applications that run on Azure.

faq.question: Can it add Copilot SDK to an existing app?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides project analysis, SDK dependency selection, route creation, authentication, and testing.

faq.question: Does it support Azure bring-your-own-model setups?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents Azure provider configuration, supported models, DefaultAzureCredential, and token refresh requirements.

faq.question: Does it execute deployments automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions for tools such as azd, Azure CLI, Docker, and curl. Users should approve commands.

faq.question: What credentials are involved?
faq.answer: Typical workflows use GitHub tokens, Azure managed identity settings, and Azure model endpoint variables.

faq.question: Is this for general Azure web apps?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on GitHub Copilot SDK services and related Azure hosting patterns.
